content:
Output went up. Cognitive load went further.

7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Look, nobody on my last team was grinding until midnight because the build was slow. The build was fine. The calendar looked civilized. The burnout came from something quieter: living all day in judgment mode without a landing gear.
You start the morning in review. You end the evening in review. Lunch is a Slack thread about whether an agent-invented migration is “probably fine.” Sleep is a half-dream about a permission scope you forgot to challenge.
That is not heroic overtime. That is a nervous system stuck on verify.
Output Metrics Hide the Load
We celebrated the charts. PRs per week up. Cycle time down. Features that used to take a sprint appeared in days. Leadership screenshots made the rounds. Someone said we had “10x’d.”
We did not chart the number of irreversible decisions compressed into each day. We did not chart how many times someone context-switched from authoring intent to policing drift. We did not chart how often a reviewer approved because another tab was waiting, not because understanding was complete.
Supervision does not feel like work in the same way coding does. It feels like anxiety with a keyboard and a green checkmark.
At a previous company we ran a week where each engineer was capped at two agent-authored PRs in flight. Throughput dipped for three days. Pages related to fresh changes dipped longer. People reported sleeping better. That should have been the loudest metric in the room. It was treated as a curiosity and then ignored.
Parallel Agents Make It Worse
One agent is a collaborator. Six agents are a distributed system where you are the only consistency protocol and you do not get Paxos, you get caffeine.
Every parallel session asks for a slice of working memory. You do not notice the cost until you try to explain any one of the changes in standup and come up with a summary that would not survive a junior’s follow-up question.
I have watched strong engineers approve a diff because the dashboard said CI was green and three other sessions were “almost done.” That is not laziness. That is queue pressure applied to a human with finite attention and a performance review that still rewards visible motion.
The fix is unfashionable: reduce concurrency until review quality recovers. Speed without comprehension is just future incident inventory with better commit messages.
What Permanent Review Mode Does to Identity
A lot of engineers liked the job because building created proof of skill. Review creates proof of vigilance. Vigilance is easier to doubt at night.
You can finish a day having prevented five bad merges and still feel like you made nothing. That emotional accounting error is how people burn out while “being productive” on paper. Managers who only reward visible generation accidentally punish the people holding the blast radius down.
There is also no clean end to the day. Coding had natural punctuation: the test passed, the branch pushed. Review mode expands to fill whatever time you give it, because there is always another diff, another agent, another “quick look.”
I started setting a hard stop: after 90 minutes of continuous review, walk. Not scroll. Walk. The quality of the next thirty minutes of judgment depended on that more than on another espresso.
Practical Guardrails That Lower the Temperature
→ One primary agent session at a time for high-risk paths; park the rest → Hard stop rules: if you cannot summarize the change in four sentences, it does not merge today → Separate “generate” blocks from “review” blocks on the calendar so your brain can change modes on purpose → Delete or park PRs older than your attention half-life instead of letting them rot into merge theater → Count prevented failures in retros, not only shipped tickets → Pair on the scariest generated diffs; solo vigilance decays under volume
None of this requires rejecting AI. It requires admitting that the human is now the rate limiter for safety, and rate limiters need cooling, redundancy, and honest capacity planning.
The Relieving Truth
If you feel more exhausted while shipping more, you are not failing at modernity. You are correctly sensing that judgment work has worse ergonomics than craft work, and the industry papered over that gap with demos and adoption graphs.
Protect the judgment budget like production capacity. Because that is what it is. Burnout here is not a personal weakness. It is what happens when a system routes all verification through people and then pretends verification is free.
Look once more at your week. If it is only custody and never craft, change the schedule before you change jobs. Sometimes the job is salvageable. Sometimes the workflow is the incident.
When the Calendar Lies
The cruel part of review-mode burnout is that your calendar looks healthy. You are not missing dinner for a deploy. You are not sleeping in the office. You are simply never leaving the cognitive posture of “is this safe to merge” while your body thinks the workday ended at six.
Partners and friends notice before managers do. You are present but unreachable. You answer “how was work” with “fine, busy” because explaining that you spent seven hours detecting absence in diffs sounds ungrateful when the team hit its sprint goal.
That social invisibility matters. Burnout without overtime is easy to dismiss — by others and by yourself. Naming it as role shape, not weakness, is the first fix.
Review Queues Need Capacity Planning Like Production.
We capacity-plan API servers. We rarely capacity-plan reviewer attention. After agents arrived, our review queue depth doubled while reviewer headcount stayed flat. Wait time grew. So did silent approvals.
Adding reviewers is not always possible. Reducing parallel agent sessions is. Splitting generation days from review days is. Treating review latency as a metric leadership watches is.
When review latency crosses a threshold, we pause new generation on tier-one paths until the queue drains. That policy felt anti-velocity the first month. Incident rate disagreed with the feeling.
What Recovery Looked Like.
Recovery was not a two-week vacation. Recovery was six weeks of calendar rules: builder mornings, review afternoons, no agent sessions after 4pm on days I carried pager. Small rules. Measurable sleep improvement. Fewer “I approved because I was tired” merges in retrospect.
If you are waiting for the company to fix this spontaneously, you may wait through a quit. Change what you can on your calendar first. Escalate with data second.
The Comparison Trap in Open Plan Teams.
You hear someone say they “shipped three features” before lunch because agents drafted while they attended meetings. You spent the morning preventing a permission bug in a generated middleware stack. Both of you look busy. Only one story sounds impressive in a standup optimized for throughput language.
That comparison erodes morale quietly. Prevention work needs explicit credit or people stop doing it thoroughly. Our retro format now includes one “prevented” item per person per sprint, optional but encouraged. The first month felt awkward. The second month people brought real examples. Review quality improved because prevention became narratable.
HR Will Send Wellness Tips.
Wellness tips are not evil. They are incomplete when the job shape is the stressor. Meditation does not shrink a review queue. A step challenge does not restore authorship.
What helped on my team was manager-visible capacity limits: if you are primary reviewer on more than three active agent-heavy PRs, you do not take a fourth until one clears. Simple rule. Enforced by the lead when individuals would not enforce it on themselves.
The Exit Interview Sentence.
A friend quit a good salary and told me the exit interview sentence: “I stopped feeling like an engineer and started feeling like a firewall with PTO.” That line stuck because it was precise. Firewalls are important. They are also not what most people signed up to become.
If that sentence resonates, change the workflow before you change employers. Many teams will adjust rules when someone credible shows the cost in rollbacks and attrition risk, not only in feelings.
A Conversation I Keep Having
A strong engineer told me they felt broken because they “could not keep up with the tools.” They were keeping up. They were drowning in verification debt created by success metrics that ignore human cognitive limits.
I asked them how many irreversible decisions they made before lunch. They counted eleven. That is not a tooling problem. That is a staffing and workflow problem wearing a productivity costume.
If your manager only asks how many agent sessions you ran, translate the conversation: ask how many merges you personally could defend in an incident review. That question restores sanity faster than another model upgrade.
